cio.name: Robert Placido (University of Maine System CIO)

ai_strategy_lead.name: Ali Abedi
ai_strategy_lead.title: Associate Vice President for Research; Director, UMaine AI Initiative

documents.title: Generative AI Teaching and Learning Guidelines

ai_strategy.summary: UMaine does not have a single published institutional AI strategy document; instead, its posture is articulated through the UMaine AI Initiative (a 40+ faculty research venture launched in 2020), the Strategic Re-Envisioning (SRE) plan's commitment to 'responsibly integrate AI' into operations, and separate teaching/learning guidelines. President Ferrini-Mundy's 2026 State of the University Address highlighted AI-enabled research (e.g., the Factory of the Future/GEM facility) as a signature investment. A system-wide AI working group delivered a report to the Board of Trustees in September 2025, and strategy work continues alongside a pending RFP for enterprise AI tooling.

key_provisions: Syllabi must specify whether AI is required, expected, minimally permitted, or forbidden
Instructors must disclose tool types, costs, and data privacy concerns
Students must acknowledge and cite all AI-generated content; failure is plagiarism
Opt-out required if AI assesses student work
Prohibits entering FERPA-protected or Personal Information into unapproved AI tools
Prohibits submitting copyrighted materials into generative AI systems
Requires participant consent before AI use in video conferences
UMS accessibility policy applies to AI interfaces (disability-equal access)
University Teaching Council reviews guidelines annually
ai_policy.summary: UMaine operates under a layered, comprehensive framework: the UMaine Generative AI Teaching and Learning Guidelines (permissive with guardrails and mandatory syllabus-level disclosure) plus UMS:IT Generative AI Guidance (October 2024) covering data categorization, FERPA protection, copyright, and accessibility. The university explicitly states it is still developing a formal institutional AI policy; existing guidance supplements the UMS Acceptable Use Policy.

bodies.name: UMaine AI Initiative Steering Committee
bodies.scope: Seven-member steering committee coordinating the 40+ faculty UMaine AI Initiative across research, education, and external partnerships

bodies.name: University Teaching Council
bodies.scope: Annual review of UMaine Generative AI Teaching and Learning Guidelines; owns ongoing guideline evolution

bodies.name: UMS System AI Working Group
bodies.scope: System-wide AI working group that delivered a public report to the Board of Trustees in September 2025; informs fee and tooling decisions across the seven campuses

bodies.name: Faculty Senate (AI Resolutions)
bodies.scope: Passed resolutions SM2605 and SM2606 (March 2026) evaluating a per-credit-hour AI fee model; prior resolutions adopted generative AI teaching guidelines

bodies.name: Maine Innovation Economy Advisory Board (MIEAB) – AI focus area
bodies.scope: Board-level advisory function highlighting AI as a priority innovation domain for the state, hosted at UMaine

governance_structure.summary: Governance is distributed: the UMaine AI Initiative and its steering committee run research/education coordination; the University Teaching Council owns pedagogical guidelines; a UMS-level AI Working Group and the Faculty Senate shape system-wide policy and pricing; the state-facing MIEAB links UMaine's AI work to Maine's innovation economy. There is no single Chief AI Officer.

initiatives.title: UMaine AI Initiative launch (2020)
initiatives.description: University-wide initiative launched in 2020 to position Maine as a 'world-class hub for AI research, education, and applications,' coordinating 40+ affiliated faculty across computing, engineering, health, social sciences, and humanities. Directed by Ali Abedi.
initiatives.significance: Establishes a coherent cross-disciplinary AI research ecosystem for the only R1 in Maine, anchoring state-level AI economic-development strategy.

investment.funding_source: UMaine (internal seed grants) + NSF + state partners
investment.description: Seed grant rounds (2020 onward) totaling over $750K disclosed; broader operating support from UMaine and leveraged federal grants.

collaborations.description: Cross-campus and cross-institutional convenings via the annual Maine AI Conference.

partners: State of Maine Office of Policy Innovation and the Future
collaborations.description: Engagement with state AI Task Force processes.

initiatives.policy_implications: Creates an institutional structure that can absorb future system-level AI policy mandates and guide faculty governance.

initiatives.title: UMaine Generative AI Teaching and Learning Guidelines
initiatives.description: Comprehensive teaching/learning guidelines requiring syllabus-level disclosure of how AI may be used (required, expected, minimally, forbidden), mandatory student citation of AI output, instructor disclosure of tool costs and privacy risks, and opt-out when AI is used to assess student work. Reviewed annually by the University Teaching Council.
initiatives.significance: One of the more explicit syllabus-level policies in the sector; binds every course to a disclosed AI posture and normalizes AI literacy as a shared instructor-student obligation.

investment.funding_source: UMaine Center for Innovation in Teaching and Learning (CITL)
investment.description: Staff effort through CITL and the University Teaching Council; recurring training and consultation resources.

collaborations.description: Aligned to UMS:IT Generative AI Guidance and UMS Acceptable Use Policy.

initiatives.policy_implications: Establishes enforceable expectations for faculty and students; provides a template other UMS campuses can adopt.

initiatives.title: UMS:IT Generative AI Guidance (October 2024)
initiatives.description: System-level guidance for faculty, staff, and students covering prohibited uses (entering Protected/Personal Information, copyrighted materials), data categorization (Restricted/Confidential/Internal under APL VI-I), and disclosure requirements. Explicitly references ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, and Google Gemini.
initiatives.significance: Extends UMaine's campus-level teaching guidelines with privacy, FERPA, and data-classification guardrails that cover administrative and research use.

investment.description: System IT staff time; integrates with existing APL and data classification framework.

collaborations.description: System-wide guidance covering UMaine, USM, UMA, UMM, UMF, UMFK, UMPI.

tools_and_vendors.name: ChatGPT
tools_and_vendors.role: Referenced as example generative AI tool under guidance

tools_and_vendors.name: Copilot
tools_and_vendors.role: Referenced as example generative AI tool under guidance

tools_and_vendors.name: Gemini
tools_and_vendors.role: Referenced as example generative AI tool under guidance

initiatives.policy_implications: Clarifies what data may not be processed through unapproved AI, shaping eventual campus-wide tool approvals.

initiatives.title: UMS per-credit-hour AI fee RFP and Faculty Senate resolutions
initiatives.description: The UMaine System issued an RFP (closed March 27, 2026) to procure enterprise AI tooling for students; Fall 2025 cost modeling estimated up to $20/credit-hour but current proposals are expected to cost 'far less.' Faculty Senate passed resolutions SM2605 and SM2606 in March 2026 recommending evaluation criteria; any fee requires Board of Trustees approval. An opt-in 'Academic Materials Program' style model is under consideration.
initiatives.significance: First large-scale institutional push to standardize and fund student AI access across a state system; a bellwether for how regional public universities price AI for students.

investment.funding_source: Potential student fee (under debate) and UMS operating funds
investment.description: Amount not disclosed; UMS signaled the approved cost will be far below the $20/credit hour Fall 2025 estimate.

collaborations.description: System-wide procurement via UMS Office of Strategic Procurement.

initiatives.policy_implications: Sets precedent for fee-based AI access at a public system; raises equity and transparency questions being actively debated by faculty and students.

initiatives.title: NSF $6M AI-in-Advanced-Manufacturing Lab
initiatives.description: National Science Foundation awarded $6 million (August 2022) to establish a new UMaine-led lab studying how AI tools and techniques can bolster advanced manufacturing in northern New England. Investigators include Liping Yu, Vikas Dhiman, John Belding, Bruce Segee, Todd Gabe, Andrew Crawley, Rebecca Colannino, and Matthew Dube.
initiatives.significance: Anchor federal investment linking AI research to Maine's manufacturing base and rural economic development; multi-disciplinary (engineering, economics, education).

investment.description: $6M over project period to launch AI-in-advanced-manufacturing lab.

collaborations.description: Regional manufacturing partners engage with the lab.

initiatives.title: Green Engineering and Materials (GEM) Building / Factory of the Future
initiatives.description: $82M, 50,000-square-foot facility (groundbreaking October 2024; completion scheduled 2026) housing the Factory of the Future with AI-enabled 3D printers, digital twin technology, real-time monitoring, AR/VR, and HPC integration. Focus: affordable housing, marine vessels, renewable energy components using bio-based materials.
initiatives.significance: Flagship AI-plus-manufacturing capital investment; positions UMaine as a national hub for AI-driven sustainable additive manufacturing.

investment.funding_source: Mix of federal, state, and UMaine funding
investment.description: ~$82M capital investment in interdisciplinary research facility.

partners: Advanced Structures and Composites Center partners
collaborations.description: Industrial partners for manufacturing, housing, marine vessels, renewable energy.

initiatives.title: Annual Maine AI Conference
initiatives.description: UMaine AI has convened the Maine AI Conference annually since 2024 at the Collins Center for the Arts in Orono. The 2nd Annual conference (June 13, 2025) used the theme 'Democratizing AI for Maine' and featured an 'unconference' format with attendee-driven topics. Panels drew from UMaine, USM, UMA, Roux Institute, Husson, Harvard, plus industry and policy experts.
initiatives.significance: Builds statewide AI community of practice across academia, industry, government; normalizes public dialogue on AI ethics, workforce, and access.

investment.description: Annual recurring event funded through UMaine AI, sponsors, and registrations.

collaborations.description: Open public engagement on AI implications for Maine.

initiatives.policy_implications: Agenda-setting forum; outputs feed Maine's state-level AI task force inputs.

initiatives.title: Learning With AI Toolkit
initiatives.description: Crowdsourced online toolkit (launched 2023) of 300+ strategies and resources for educators and students navigating generative AI. Co-created by Jon Ippolito (Professor of New Media) with UMaine's New Media program and the Center for Innovation in Teaching and Learning (CITL). Functions as a sortable 'cookbook' of pedagogical tactics.
initiatives.significance: Internationally recognized faculty resource; illustrates how a humanities-forward R1 can lead in AI literacy and pedagogical critique.

investment.description: Staff time and volunteer contributions; crowdsourced community.

collaborations.description: Contributions solicited across UMS and international academic community.

initiatives.policy_implications: Operationalizes UMaine's teaching/learning guidelines into concrete classroom recipes.

initiatives.title: Engineering Applications of AI Graduate and Undergraduate Certificates
initiatives.description: Only college in Maine to offer a graduate certificate covering engineering applications of AI — 12 credits covering smart manufacturing, Industry 4.0, autonomous vehicles, smart grids, and IoT. Paired online undergraduate certificate provides foundational AI engineering skills. Delivered through UMaine Online.
initiatives.significance: Translates UMaine AI research strengths into workforce credentials; closes a state-level gap in AI-specific post-secondary programs.

investment.funding_source: UMaine Online; Maine College of Engineering and Computing
investment.description: Tuition-funded certificate programs with dedicated faculty.

initiatives.title: ARCSIM Penobscot HPC cluster and AI compute
initiatives.description: Advanced Research Computing, Security & Information Management (ARCSIM) operates the Penobscot HPC cluster, which includes an NVIDIA DGX A100 (8 x 40GB), two 80GB A100s, three L40s, four A30s, and eight RTX 2080 Ti GPUs on 1TB-RAM nodes, plus 14 Dell AMD EPYC Milan nodes (96 cores/node). Open OnDemand web access; RStudio/Jupyter/Matlab support. ARCSIM partners with TACC, Ohio Supercomputer Center.
initiatives.significance: Provides dedicated, high-memory GPU infrastructure for AI and ML research that rural R1s often lack; underpins UMaine's AI research portfolio.

investment.funding_source: UMaine Office of the Vice President for Research; federal/state supplements
investment.description: Operating budget plus capital for GPU refresh; specific expenditure not publicly disclosed.

collaborations.description: Federated access to national supercomputing resources.

initiatives.title: Roux Institute / Northeastern University research partnership seed grants
initiatives.description: UMaine and Northeastern's Roux Institute (Portland, ME) signed a research agreement in 2020 and have since awarded multiple rounds of joint seed grants to collaborative research teams in AI, health, climate, manufacturing, and marine science.
initiatives.significance: Formalizes a private-public R1 alliance that strengthens Maine's AI research density; shares faculty, students, and infrastructure across two campuses.

investment.funding_source: UMaine + Northeastern / Roux Institute matching seed funds
investment.description: Multiple rounds of joint seed funding for paired research teams.

collaborations.description: Cross-institution partnership with joint solicitations and awarded teams.

initiatives.title: NSF EPSCoR Maine-FOREST (AI for forest economy)
initiatives.description: NSF EPSCoR $7M Track-1 award (Maine-FOREST / Forest-based Opportunities for Resilient Economy, Sustainability and Technology) uses AI and informatics to model and sustain Maine's forest sector. Involves UMaine, USM, UMFK, UMPI, Colby, Bates, and the Maine Development Foundation. Supports 45+ faculty, 85 undergrads, 10 grad students, and 4 postdocs.
initiatives.significance: Applies AI to Maine's dominant rural industry; aligns with state Wabanaki resilience priorities.

partners: University of Maine at Fort Kent
University of Maine at Presque Isle

initiatives.title: AI and Digital Twin Internships for the Blue Economy
initiatives.description: Announced November 12, 2025: UMaine launched a three-year program to support 48 undergraduate and graduate students through eight-week summer and year-round internships building AI-powered digital twins of ocean structures for offshore aquaculture, autonomous shipping, and marine renewable energy. Students work with UMaine ocean test beds and faculty labs.
initiatives.significance: Directly pairs AI/ML skill-building with Maine's marine economy; a replicable model for aligning workforce pipelines with regional industry needs.

investment.funding_source: Federal and state support (not fully disclosed)
investment.description: Three-year funded program; dollar figure not publicly itemized.

initiatives.title: Sekeh Lab AI safety and adversarial robustness research
initiatives.description: Assistant Professor Salimeh Yasaei Sekeh leads the Sekeh Lab (2022 NSF CAREER Award; 2023 MCEC Early Career Research Award) researching reliable and safe AI, deep learning efficiency, adversarial attack detection, and model robustness. A funded project targets 'Improved Adversarial Attack Detection Toward Robustness of Deep Neural Networks.'
initiatives.significance: Brings AI safety and robustness research into UMaine's portfolio, relevant to sector-wide concerns about trustworthy AI.

investment.description: NSF CAREER award (~$500K typical range) plus internal MCEC recognition.

computing_infrastructure.summary: ARCSIM operates the Penobscot HPC cluster, the institution's primary AI/ML compute platform. GPU inventory includes a NVIDIA DGX A100 (8 x A100 40GB), 2 x A100 80GB, 3 x L40, 4 x A30, and 8 x RTX 2080 Ti — roughly 25 AI-capable GPUs. CPU side includes 14 Dell AMD EPYC Milan nodes (96 cores, 512GB RAM each) plus high-memory and Intel nodes. Infiniband HDR100 interconnect; Open OnDemand web access. Federated access to TACC and Ohio Supercomputer Center.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: UMS ICT Accessibility Policy applied to generative AI
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: System-wide policy explicitly extends accessibility requirements to generative AI interfaces and outputs (disability-equal access).

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: Democratizing AI for Maine (2025 Maine AI Conference theme)
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: Statewide convening framed around equitable AI access across rural communities, Wabanaki partners, and small businesses.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: Maine-FOREST rural and Wabanaki resilience thread
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: NSF EPSCoR project explicitly includes rural and Wabanaki resilience as a core focus, using AI to support underrepresented Maine communities.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: UMS AI fee equity debate
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: Faculty Senate resolutions SM2605/SM2606 raise explicit equity concerns about any per-credit-hour AI fee model and recommend opt-in structures.

regulatory_context.summary: Maine Governor Janet Mills signed Executive Order establishing the Maine Artificial Intelligence Task Force on December 20, 2024; the Task Force issued its public report in October/November 2025 covering economy, workforce, education, and consumer protection. Maine's state generative AI policy (Office of Information Technology) governs state agency use. The University of Maine System issued UMS:IT Generative AI Guidance (October 2024) with system-wide data-privacy requirements; the UMS Acceptable Use Policy applies to all AI use. State-level data center restrictions have been debated and may affect large-scale AI infrastructure siting in Maine. Maine enacted a law in 2024 requiring limited disclosures of AI technology in consumer interactions.

controversies_and_litigation.summary: Faculty Senate (SM2605/SM2606) pushed back on UMS plans for a per-credit-hour AI fee; ongoing debate over cost, equity, and student opt-out.

narrative_summary: The University of Maine is Maine's only R1 and anchors the state's AI posture. Its institutional strategy is not a single published document but a distributed architecture: the UMaine AI Initiative (launched 2020 under Associate VP for Research Ali Abedi; 40+ affiliated faculty across engineering, computing, health, humanities, and social sciences), the Strategic Re-Envisioning plan's 'responsibly integrate AI' commitment, and the forthcoming UMS system-wide AI tooling selection. President Joan Ferrini-Mundy and Provost Gabriel Paquette lead academic direction; the CIO role resides at the UMaine System level (Robert Placido). There is no named Chief AI Officer. On policy, UMaine operates a comprehensive two-layer framework: syllabus-level Generative AI Teaching and Learning Guidelines (annually reviewed by the University Teaching Council) and October 2024 UMS:IT Generative AI Guidance on data privacy, FERPA, copyright, and accessibility. Governance is shared among the UMaine AI Initiative Steering Committee, the University Teaching Council, the Faculty Senate, and the UMS AI Working Group. Research depth is evident: a $6M NSF lab in AI for advanced manufacturing, $7M NSF EPSCoR Maine-FOREST using AI for forest economy resilience, the $82M Green Engineering and Materials building housing the AI-enabled Factory of the Future (opening 2026), and named labs including SKAI, MaineSAIL, Sekeh Lab (AI safety), VEMI, and WiSe Net. ARCSIM's Penobscot HPC cluster (DGX A100 plus multiple A100/L40/A30 GPUs) provides meaningful AI compute for a rural R1. Statewide, UMaine convenes the annual Maine AI Conference and is formally partnered with Northeastern's Roux Institute; it participates in the state's AI Task Force process. Student-facing AI is modest: the Learning With AI toolkit (Jon Ippolito, CITL) and engineering AI certificates; no confirmed enterprise chatbot or campus-wide generative AI deployment yet. The biggest open question is the pending UMS per-credit-hour AI fee — an equity, access, and vendor-selection debate actively playing out in the Faculty Senate and Board of Trustees through early 2026. Gaps: no named CIO at the campus level, no public campus-level AI strategy document, and no confirmed enterprise Copilot/ChatGPT Edu/Gemini contract despite guidance referencing all three.

gaps_acknowledged: No confirmed enterprise-scale AI tool deployment (Copilot/ChatGPT Edu/Gemini); UMS guidance references them but a campus-wide contract is not publicly announced.
No named campus-level CIO separate from the UMS CIO; unclear whether UMaine has a dedicated CIO or relies entirely on system IT leadership.

No published institutional AI strategy document distinct from the UMaine AI Initiative vision; references to 'responsibly integrate AI' in the Strategic Re-Envisioning plan not located as a standalone policy.
No confirmed student-facing AI chatbot or predictive-analytics retention product publicly documented at UMaine.
Specific dollar figures for several UMaine AI investments (seed grant totals beyond the $750K cumulative figure; Penobscot HPC capital outlay) are not publicly disclosed.
Founding year for Sekeh Lab listed as 2020 based on Sekeh's UMaine start; exact lab founding date not confirmed.
